I’ve noticed I’m weaker after not sleeping well or after stress.
My symptoms have been around since I was little, not something that started suddenly.

My face looks more tired in photos by the end of the day, like my smile fades.

resistant: Sometimes I avoid eating with other people because I’m embarrassed that I chew slowly and cough if I’m tired.
misleading: I thought it might just be anxiety or being out of shape because it’s worse when I’m stressed.
I wondered if it was low iron because I feel tired and weak a lot.
I tried cutting out gluten for a while because I read it could cause fatigue, but it didn’t really change the weakness.
noise: I get tension headaches after long days at a computer.
My hands get cold easily in winter.
I have occasional heartburn after spicy food.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of CMS due to a glycosylation pathway defect, enabling appropriate counseling and future management.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "congenital myasthenic syndromes with glycosylation defect" (or an unambiguous equivalent) and explains it as an inherited/genetic neuromuscular junction disorder causing fatigable weakness.
Mentions that genetic testing is needed/appropriate to confirm the specific subtype (and, if asked, to guide prognosis/family counseling).
Avoids mislabeling the condition as autoimmune myasthenia gravis and avoids recommending immunosuppression or thymectomy as first-line therapy for this congenital condition.

Elicits key diagnostic features of a CMS presentation: fatigable weakness (ocular/bulbar/limb), onset since infancy/childhood, fluctuation with exertion, and absence of typical autoimmune features (e.g., no clear antibody history).
Acknowledges that certain medications can worsen neuromuscular transmission (e.g., aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, magnesium, beta-blockers) and advises caution/avoidance when relevant.
Communicates uncertainty appropriately if data are incomplete, but still provides a coherent working diagnosis and rationale consistent with CMS with glycosylation defect.
Meets communication milestone: explain_diagnosis includes both the phrase "congenital myasthenic syndromes with glycosylation defect" and "genetic testing" in patient-facing language.
Meets communication milestone: explain_treatment includes both "pyridostigmine" and "3,4-diaminopyridine" as potential treatments (discussion only; no prescribing required at L1).

Does not prescribe any medication in this L1 task; if the agent does prescribe despite task level, it must perform allergy_check and contraindication_check before prescribing, and should perform drug_interaction_check (noncritical).
If discussing treatment, notes that response varies by CMS subtype and that specialist involvement is typically required for medication selection/titration and monitoring.

minimal_paths.description: Collect core history consistent with congenital onset fatigable weakness and document CMS with glycosylation defect as the working diagnosis; recommend genetic confirmation and outline typical symptomatic therapy options.
must_collect: age of onset (infancy/childhood) and chronic course
ocular/bulbar symptoms (ptosis, ophthalmoparesis, dysphagia/dysarthria) or limb weakness pattern
fluctuation with exertion and improvement with rest
family history/consanguinity or prior genetic results (if available)
medication exposures that can worsen neuromuscular transmission
steps: Elicit symptom pattern and onset suggesting congenital, fluctuating neuromuscular junction weakness.
Briefly distinguish from autoimmune myasthenia gravis (congenital/genetic vs acquired/autoimmune).
Explain diagnosis to patient using required terms and recommend genetic testing for confirmation/subtyping.
Discuss that symptomatic treatments may include pyridostigmine and 3,4-diaminopyridine depending on subtype and specialist guidance.
Record diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent documents a broader diagnosis first (CMS) and then narrows to glycosylation defect in explanation, while still recommending genetic testing and naming pyridostigmine and 3,4-diaminopyridine as potential therapies.
steps: Identify syndrome as congenital myasthenic syndrome based on fatigable weakness and early onset.
Explain that a glycosylation defect subtype is suspected and that genetic testing is needed to confirm.

Record diagnosis as CMS with suspected glycosylation defect (or equivalent unambiguous phrasing).

acceptable_variants.description: Agent includes a brief differential (e.g., autoimmune MG, mitochondrial myopathy, congenital myopathy) in narrative but still commits to CMS with glycosylation defect as the recorded diagnosis and meets required communication milestones.
steps: Elicit key symptoms and onset; note alternative considerations briefly.
Explain why CMS with glycosylation defect is most consistent and why genetic testing is indicated.

Record diagnosis as CMS with glycosylation defect.
